danger_signals.visual_form: alien bandit ship patterns moving in coordinated waves across screen
danger_signals.meaning: active hostile threat requiring player engagement or evasion

danger_signals.visual_form: derelict or damaged spacecraft visuals in environment
danger_signals.meaning: environmental hazard and cover for enemy forces; structural danger

danger_signals.visual_form: unspecified unknown destructive phenomena in Delta Sector space
danger_signals.meaning: ambient environmental danger; world-state threat beyond faction conflict

danger_signals.visual_form: disabled/unavailable power-up containers rendered in gray coloration
danger_signals.meaning: collision hazard; player death if contacted; visual distinction from active pickups

danger_signals.visual_form: specific enemy formation visual patterns that subtract power-up points
danger_signals.meaning: enemies that weaken player capability; tactical threat requiring identification

safety_signals.visual_form: colored (non-gray) container blocks appearing periodically in play space
safety_signals.meaning: safe to collect; provides temporary capability enhancement (speed, fire rate)
safe state achieved; credits earned; progression moment

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Delta Sector badge/insignia (not detailed in source)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: player-controlled battle cruiser serves as faction visual representative; elite police squadron aesthetic

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: formation patterns distinguish positive-value enemies from negative-value enemies visually
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: use lost ships and environmental hazards as tactical cover/concealment

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: continuous scrolling environment filled with enemy formations, lost ship debris, unknown destructive forces
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemies spawn in coordinated waves requiring player destruction or evasion; hazards persist

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: power-up effect timers expire; UI/gameplay feedback shows capability loss
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: weapon fire rate and ship speed return to baseline; player must re-engage with power-up system

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: wave completion screen; credit accumulation display
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: unlock access to weapon/ship modification menus; increased purchasing power for upgrades

environmental_storytelling.example: derelict spacecraft scattered through Delta Sector serve as both environmental hazards and narrative evidence of prior conflict/catastrophe
alien bandits use lost ships and unknown forces as tactical cover, visually communicating asymmetric warfare and adaptation to sector environment
temporary power-up effects that degrade over time imply resource scarcity and survival pressure in Delta Sector operations

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: move in coordinated patterns; worth destroying for point accumulation
visually distinguishable from positive formations; destroying them penalizes player; requires tactical identification before engagement
utilize lost ships and environmental phenomena as concealment; ambush-oriented threat model
remain in play space but kill player on contact; visual grayout signals danger rather than opportunity

notes: Delta (1987) uses minimal faction/political visual language. The core danger/safety communication occurs through enemy formation recognition and power-up coloration. World-state is primarily communicated through mission progression (wave completion = safety moment) and capability decay (temporary power-up duration). The 'Delta Patrol' vs 'alien bandits' conflict is thematic rather than visually complex; no detailed faction insignia, color schemes, or political symbolism documented. Environmental storytelling relies on lost ships as danger markers and narrative evidence. The game's visual communication strategy is functionally oriented toward moment-to-moment threat identification and resource management rather than world-state nuance.
